questions.question: The transformer architecture replaced recurrence with attention. What practical advantage does this give over RNNs when processing a sequence?
options: It processes all positions in parallel and models long-range dependencies directly via attention
It can only process one token at a time, like an RNN
It removes the need for any learned parameters
It guarantees the sequence length is always fixed
questions.explanation: Because attention relates every position to every other position in one operation, transformers process tokens in parallel (no sequential recurrence) and connect distant tokens directly. RNNs must step through the sequence and struggle to carry long-range signal.

questions.question: In an attention layer, each token produces a Query, Key, and Value. Conceptually, what determines how much one token attends to another?
options: The alphabetical order of the tokens
Only the token's position, never its content
The similarity (dot product) between one token's Query and another token's Key, normalized by softmax
The size of the vocabulary
questions.explanation: Attention weights come from comparing a Query against all Keys via dot products, scaled and passed through softmax. The resulting weights are used to combine the Values. So content-based Query-Key similarity drives how much attention one token pays to another.

questions.question: Multi-head attention runs several attention computations in parallel with different projections. Why is this better than a single attention head?
options: It reduces the model to a single representation subspace
It makes attention non-differentiable for stability
It eliminates the softmax operation
Different heads can attend to different relationships/positions, letting the model capture multiple types of dependencies at once
questions.explanation: Each head projects Q/K/V into its own subspace and learns to focus on different patterns (e.g. syntactic vs. positional relations). Concatenating heads gives the model a richer, multi-faceted view than a single head's averaged attention.

questions.question: BERT is described as a bidirectional encoder, while GPT is a left-to-right decoder. What pretraining objective makes BERT bidirectional?
options: Next-token prediction reading strictly left to right
Masked language modeling, predicting randomly masked tokens using both left and right context
Translating English to French
Predicting the image that matches a caption
questions.explanation: BERT uses masked language modeling: random tokens are masked and the model predicts them using context from both directions. This bidirectional conditioning contrasts with GPT's autoregressive next-token objective, which only sees prior tokens.

questions.question: GPT-style models are autoregressive. During text generation, how do they produce a sequence?
options: They predict one token at a time conditioned on all previously generated tokens, feeding each output back as input
They output the whole sequence at once in a single forward pass with no ordering
They fill in randomly masked positions simultaneously
They require the full target sequence as input to generate
questions.explanation: Autoregressive generation predicts the next token given the tokens so far, then appends it and repeats. A causal mask prevents attending to future positions, so each step depends only on previously generated context.

questions.question: In the original encoder-decoder transformer, the decoder uses two kinds of attention. Besides masked self-attention over its own outputs, what is the role of the cross-attention sublayer?
options: It re-encodes the source sentence from scratch
It removes positional information from the input
It lets each decoder position attend over the encoder's output representations of the source sequence
It computes the final loss directly
questions.explanation: Cross-attention (encoder-decoder attention) uses the decoder's queries against the encoder's keys and values, so each generated position can focus on the relevant parts of the source. This is how the decoder conditions its output on the encoded input in tasks like translation.

questions.question: Because self-attention is permutation-invariant, transformers add positional encodings. What problem would arise without them?
options: The model would run out of memory
The vocabulary would double in size
Attention weights could not be computed at all
The model would treat 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' as identical, having no sense of token order
questions.explanation: Self-attention alone has no notion of order, so without positional information the model cannot distinguish word orderings. Positional encodings inject each token's position into its embedding, letting the model use sequence order to disambiguate meaning.

questions.question: Transfer learning with pretrained transformers means you start from a model pretrained on massive text, then fine-tune on your smaller task dataset. Why is this so effective for a small dataset?
options: The pretrained weights are discarded, so only your data matters
Pretraining already learned broad language representations, so fine-tuning needs far less task-specific data to reach strong performance
It removes the need for any labels in your task
It only works if your dataset is larger than the pretraining corpus
questions.explanation: Pretraining on huge corpora gives the model general linguistic knowledge. Fine-tuning adapts those representations to your task, so even a small labeled dataset can yield strong results, since the model is not learning language from scratch.

questions.question: You install the Hugging Face stack and want to download and run a pretrained model in just a few lines. Which abstraction gives the quickest path for a standard task like sentiment analysis?
options: The `pipeline()` API, which bundles tokenizer, model, and post-processing for a task
Manually implementing the transformer math from scratch
Training a model from random initialization
Editing the model's config file by hand
questions.explanation: `pipeline('sentiment-analysis')` (and similar) loads an appropriate pretrained model and tokenizer and handles pre/post-processing, returning predictions from raw text in a couple of lines. It is the fastest way to apply a model to a common task.

questions.question: Using `AutoModel.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')` and `AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')`, why must the tokenizer match the model checkpoint?
options: Any tokenizer works with any model
The tokenizer only affects printing, not the model
The model expects token ids from the exact vocabulary and tokenization scheme it was trained with; a mismatch produces meaningless inputs
Matching is only needed for image models
questions.explanation: Each checkpoint was trained with a specific vocabulary and tokenization. The model interprets token ids relative to that vocabulary, so using a mismatched tokenizer feeds it ids that mean something different, corrupting predictions. Always pair the model's own tokenizer.

questions.question: A model trained on English encounters the rare word 'antidisestablishmentarianism' it never saw whole. How does subword tokenization (e.g. BPE/WordPiece) handle it gracefully?
options: It maps the whole word to a single unknown token
It refuses to process the sentence
It deletes the word from the input
It splits the word into known subword units so the model can still represent it
questions.explanation: Subword tokenizers build a vocabulary of frequent fragments, so an unseen word is broken into known sub-units rather than collapsed to a single unknown token. This keeps a useful representation and dramatically reduces out-of-vocabulary problems.

questions.question: Fine-tuning BERT for text classification, you use `AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased', num_labels=3)`. What does this do beyond loading BERT's encoder?
options: It freezes the entire model so nothing trains
It attaches a fresh, randomly initialized classification head sized to 3 labels on top of the pretrained encoder
It converts BERT into a text generator
It deletes BERT's pretrained weights
questions.explanation: The ForSequenceClassification variant adds a new classification head (a linear layer over the pooled representation) with the specified number of labels. The pretrained encoder weights are kept and fine-tuned, while the head is learned from scratch for your task.

questions.question: Named Entity Recognition is a token-classification task. How does the model produce its output, and why is label alignment with subword tokens important?
options: It predicts a label per token, so labels must be aligned to subword pieces (often labeling only the first sub-token of a word)
It outputs one label for the whole sentence; subwords are irrelevant
It generates free-form text describing the entities
It returns a single yes/no for whether any entity exists
questions.explanation: NER assigns a tag (e.g. B-PER, I-LOC, O) to each token. Since the tokenizer splits words into subwords, labels from word-level annotations must be aligned to those pieces — commonly tagging the first sub-token and marking the rest as ignored — to train correctly.

questions.question: In extractive question answering on SQuAD, what does the model actually predict to answer a question?
options: It generates a brand-new sentence not present in the context
It classifies the question into one of several categories
It predicts the start and end token positions of the answer span within the provided context
It returns the entire context unchanged
questions.explanation: Extractive QA models output two distributions over context tokens — one for the start position and one for the end — and the answer is the span between the most likely start and end. The answer must be a contiguous substring of the given context.

questions.question: For semantic similarity, why are sentence-embedding models (e.g. Sentence-BERT style) preferred over feeding both sentences through BERT and comparing raw [CLS] vectors for large-scale search?
options: They cannot compute similarity at all
They only work for single-word inputs
They require running the full model on every sentence pair every time
They produce fixed embeddings per sentence so you can precompute and compare with cosine similarity, scaling to many comparisons efficiently
questions.explanation: Sentence-embedding models map each sentence to a single dense vector independently, so embeddings can be precomputed once and compared cheaply with cosine similarity. Cross-encoding every pair through BERT is far more accurate per pair but does not scale to large retrieval sets.

questions.question: When you call a Hugging Face tokenizer on a batch of sentences of different lengths with `padding=True, truncation=True`, what does it ensure, and what extra output handles the padding?
options: It removes all punctuation and returns only token ids
It pads short and truncates long sequences to a uniform length, and returns an attention mask marking real versus padded tokens
It translates the text to English first
It deletes sentences longer than three words
questions.explanation: Padding adds pad tokens to short sequences and truncation cuts overly long ones, producing a rectangular batch within the model's maximum length. The accompanying attention mask flags real versus padded positions so the model ignores padding when computing attention.

questions.question: Building a complete NLP pipeline, you must split your labeled data before fine-tuning. Why fit any preprocessing (like a label encoder or vectorizer statistics) on the training split only?
options: Fitting on validation/test data leaks information into training, inflating evaluation metrics and giving an over-optimistic view
It makes training run faster
Validation data is never used for evaluation
Preprocessing must always use the entire dataset
questions.explanation: Any statistic learned from data is part of the model and must not see the evaluation splits, or information leaks across the boundary and metrics become optimistic. Fit preprocessing on train, then apply the fitted transform to validation and test.

questions.question: Writing a custom PyTorch training loop for a transformer, you call `loss.backward()` then `optimizer.step()`. Before the next iteration you must also call:
options: `model.eval()` every step
`torch.no_grad()` around the whole loop
`optimizer.zero_grad()`, because PyTorch accumulates gradients by default
`model.save_pretrained()` after each batch
questions.explanation: PyTorch adds new gradients to the existing `.grad` buffers rather than overwriting them. Without `optimizer.zero_grad()` each iteration, gradients from previous batches accumulate and corrupt the update, so you must clear them before the next backward pass.

questions.question: Training a large transformer, you enable mixed precision (fp16). What is the main benefit, and what guards against numerical issues?
options: It increases accuracy with no downsides and needs no safeguards
It removes the need for a GPU
It converts the model to integer-only arithmetic
It cuts memory use and speeds compute; loss/gradient scaling guards small fp16 gradients from underflowing to zero
questions.explanation: Mixed precision stores activations/gradients in fp16 to save memory and exploit fast tensor cores, keeping critical computations in higher precision. Loss scaling multiplies the loss before backprop so tiny gradients stay representable in fp16, preventing underflow.

questions.question: To reduce inference latency of a deployed transformer without retraining from scratch, you apply dynamic quantization to int8. What trade-off should you expect?
options: Larger model with higher accuracy
Smaller model and faster inference, typically with a small drop in accuracy
No change in size but slower inference
It only works during training, not inference
questions.explanation: Quantizing weights (and sometimes activations) to int8 shrinks the model and speeds up integer arithmetic at inference, especially on CPU. The cost is some loss of numerical precision, usually a minor accuracy decrease that is acceptable for the latency gains.

questions.question: Optimizing inference speed, you batch many short requests together and use a smaller distilled model (e.g. DistilBERT). Why does distillation help here?
options: A distilled student model is smaller and faster while retaining much of the teacher's accuracy, lowering latency and cost
Distilled models are always more accurate than their teachers
Distillation increases the parameter count for speed
It only changes the tokenizer
questions.explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a compact student to mimic a larger teacher, yielding a model with far fewer parameters that runs faster and uses less memory while keeping most of the teacher's quality, ideal for latency- and cost-sensitive serving.

questions.question: You ask a large language model to classify text into a label it was never explicitly fine-tuned on, just by describing the labels in the prompt. What is this called?
options: Supervised fine-tuning
Gradient descent
Zero-shot learning, where the model performs a task with no task-specific training examples
Tokenization
questions.explanation: Zero-shot learning has the model perform a task it was not trained on, guided only by a natural-language description (or candidate labels). It leverages broad pretrained knowledge rather than task-specific labeled examples or weight updates.

questions.question: In few-shot in-context learning, you place a handful of input-output examples directly in the prompt. How does the model use them?
options: It updates its weights based on the examples
It ignores the examples entirely
It stores the examples permanently in the tokenizer
It conditions on the examples at inference to infer the pattern and apply it to the new query, without any weight updates
questions.explanation: In-context learning uses the demonstrations within the prompt as context to recognize the desired pattern and produce a matching answer for the query. No gradients or parameter changes occur; the conditioning happens purely through the forward pass on the prompt.

questions.question: A prompt-engineering technique adds 'Let's think step by step' or worked reasoning to improve performance on multi-step problems. What is this approach generally called?
options: Quantization
Chain-of-thought prompting, which elicits intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer
Batch normalization
Tokenization
questions.explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting encourages the model to generate intermediate reasoning steps rather than jumping to an answer, which often improves accuracy on arithmetic, logic, and multi-step tasks by structuring the computation the model performs.

questions.question: Multi-task learning trains one transformer to handle several tasks at once (e.g. classification and NER) with a shared encoder. What is a key benefit?
options: Shared representations can transfer knowledge between related tasks and improve data efficiency versus training each task alone
Each task must use a completely separate model
It guarantees every task reaches identical accuracy
It removes the need for task-specific outputs
questions.explanation: A shared encoder learns features useful across tasks, so related tasks can reinforce each other (positive transfer) and the model uses parameters and data more efficiently than separate single-task models. Task-specific heads still produce each task's outputs.

questions.question: Among advanced optimization techniques for transformers, gradient accumulation is used when GPU memory cannot fit a large batch. How does it simulate a larger effective batch size?
options: It increases the GPU's physical memory
It reduces the model to half its layers
It runs several forward/backward passes on small micro-batches, summing their gradients before a single optimizer step
It skips the backward pass entirely
questions.explanation: Gradient accumulation processes several small micro-batches, accumulating their gradients without stepping the optimizer, then performs one update after N micro-batches. This yields the same effective batch size as a large batch while keeping peak memory low.

questions.question: Standard self-attention has O(n^2) cost in sequence length n, making very long contexts expensive. What do efficient/long-context transformers (e.g. sparse or linear attention) primarily change?
options: They remove attention entirely and use only convolutions
They double the quadratic cost for accuracy
They shorten every input to 8 tokens
They approximate or sparsify attention so cost scales sub-quadratically, enabling much longer sequences
questions.explanation: The quadratic memory/time of full attention limits context length. Efficient transformers use sparse patterns, low-rank, or kernel/linear approximations so the cost grows roughly linearly or n-log-n, allowing the model to handle much longer documents.

questions.question: You fine-tuned a model and want to deploy it via the Hugging Face Hub so others can load it with `from_pretrained`. What must you push alongside the model weights?
options: Only the weights file by itself
The config and the tokenizer files too, so the repository is self-contained and loadable
Your entire training dataset
A compiled C++ binary
questions.explanation: A loadable Hub repo needs the model weights, the configuration (architecture/hyperparameters), and the tokenizer files. With all of these, anyone can call from_pretrained on the repo id and reconstruct both the model and its matching tokenization.

questions.question: Monitoring a deployed model, you notice accuracy on live traffic slowly declining over months although the code is unchanged. What is the most likely cause and response?
options: Data/concept drift — the input distribution shifted from training; monitor inputs/predictions and retrain on recent data
A code bug; rewrite the inference function
The model file corrupted itself; re-download it
The server clock is wrong; restart it
questions.explanation: Silent accuracy decay with unchanged code typically means the live data distribution has drifted away from the training distribution. Monitoring detects it, and retraining (or updating) the model on fresh, representative data restores performance.

questions.question: Choosing an evaluation metric for an imbalanced text-classification deployment where the positive class is rare, why is accuracy a poor primary metric?
options: Accuracy cannot be computed on text
Accuracy always equals recall
A model predicting the majority class can score high accuracy while failing the rare class; F1/precision/recall reflect real performance
Accuracy requires a generative model
questions.explanation: With class imbalance, always predicting the majority class yields high accuracy yet is useless for the rare class. Precision, recall, and F1 (or PR-AUC) focus on the minority class and reveal whether the model is actually catching the cases that matter.

questions.question: In the capstone, you build a domain-specific transformer application. You have limited in-domain labeled data but lots of unlabeled in-domain text. Which strategy best leverages both?
options: Train a transformer from random initialization on only the labeled data
Use a smaller vocabulary to ignore domain terms
Discard the unlabeled text since it has no labels
Continue pretraining (domain-adaptive) on the unlabeled in-domain text, then fine-tune on the labeled data
questions.explanation: Domain-adaptive pretraining continues the masked-language-modeling objective on unlabeled in-domain text so the model learns domain vocabulary and style, then supervised fine-tuning on the scarce labels adapts it to the task. This uses both data sources far better than training from scratch on labels alone.

questions.question: Replacing recurrence with attention lets transformers, compared to RNNs:
options: Process all positions in parallel and model long-range dependencies directly
Process one token at a time
Drop all learned parameters
Require a fixed sequence length
questions.explanation: Attention relates every position to every other in one operation, enabling parallel processing and direct long-range connections that sequential RNNs lack.

questions.question: How much one token attends to another is determined by:
options: Alphabetical order
Position only, never content
Query-Key dot-product similarity normalized by softmax
Vocabulary size
questions.explanation: Attention weights come from scaled Query-Key dot products through softmax, then combine the Values; content-based similarity drives attention.

questions.question: Multi-head attention beats a single head because heads:
options: Collapse to one subspace
Make attention non-differentiable
Remove softmax
Attend to different relationships/positions, capturing multiple dependency types at once
questions.explanation: Each head projects into its own subspace and focuses on different patterns; concatenating them gives a richer, multi-faceted representation.

questions.question: BERT is bidirectional because its pretraining objective is:
options: Strict left-to-right next-token prediction
Masked language modeling, predicting masked tokens from both-side context
English-to-French translation
Caption-image matching
questions.explanation: Masked language modeling predicts randomly masked tokens using left and right context, giving bidirectional conditioning unlike GPT's autoregressive objective.

questions.question: Subword tokenization (BPE/WordPiece) handles an unseen rare word by:
options: Splitting it into known subword units so it can still be represented
Mapping it to a single unknown token
Deleting it
Refusing the sentence
questions.explanation: Subword vocabularies break unseen words into known fragments instead of one unknown token, preserving a representation and reducing OOV issues.

questions.question: Without positional encodings, a transformer would:
options: Run out of memory
Be unable to compute attention
Treat 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' as identical, lacking order information
Double its vocabulary
questions.explanation: Self-attention is permutation-invariant; positional encodings inject order so the model can distinguish different word orderings.

questions.question: Fine-tuning a pretrained transformer is effective on a small dataset because:
options: Pretrained weights are discarded
Your data must exceed the pretraining corpus
No labels are required
Pretraining already learned broad language representations, so little task data is needed
questions.explanation: The model already knows general language from pretraining; fine-tuning only adapts it, so small labeled sets can reach strong performance.

questions.question: Using AutoModel and AutoTokenizer, the tokenizer must match the checkpoint because:
options: Any tokenizer works with any model
The model interprets token ids relative to its trained vocabulary; a mismatch yields meaningless inputs
The tokenizer only affects printing
Matching matters only for image models
questions.explanation: Each checkpoint was trained with a specific vocabulary and scheme; a mismatched tokenizer feeds ids that mean something else, corrupting predictions.

questions.question: Calling a tokenizer with padding=True, truncation=True on variable-length sentences:
options: Pads short and truncates long sequences to a uniform length the model accepts
Removes punctuation and lowercases
Translates to English
Drops sentences over three words
questions.explanation: Padding/truncation produce a rectangular batch within max length, and an attention mask marks real vs padded tokens so padding doesn't affect output.

questions.question: AutoModelForSequenceClassification(..., num_labels=3) on BERT:
options: Freezes everything
Turns BERT into a generator
Adds a fresh randomly initialized 3-label classification head atop the pretrained encoder
Deletes pretrained weights
questions.explanation: It attaches a new linear classification head sized to the labels while keeping and fine-tuning the pretrained encoder; the head is learned from scratch.

questions.question: Extractive QA on SQuAD answers a question by predicting:
options: A brand-new sentence not in the context
The whole context unchanged
A question category
The start and end token positions of the answer span within the context
questions.explanation: The model outputs start and end distributions over context tokens; the answer is the contiguous span between the most likely start and end.

questions.question: NER is token classification, so subword label alignment matters because:
options: It outputs one sentence-level label
It predicts a label per token, so word labels must align to subword pieces (often labeling the first sub-token)
It generates free-form text
It returns a single yes/no
questions.explanation: NER tags each token; since words split into subwords, word-level labels must be aligned to those pieces to train correctly.

questions.question: Translation uses an encoder-decoder rather than BERT because:
options: The encoder represents the source and the decoder autoregressively generates the target conditioned on it
Encoder-only models generate more fluently
BERT is always faster
Translation is just classification
questions.explanation: Seq2seq maps variable-length source to target; the decoder generates tokens attending to the encoder output. BERT lacks the generative decoder needed.

questions.question: In a custom PyTorch loop, before the next iteration after backward/step you must call:
options: model.eval() each step
torch.no_grad() around the loop
optimizer.zero_grad(), since PyTorch accumulates gradients by default
save_pretrained() per batch
questions.explanation: PyTorch adds gradients to existing buffers; without zeroing them each step, stale gradients accumulate and corrupt updates.

questions.question: Mixed-precision (fp16) training mainly gives lower memory and faster compute, safeguarded by:
options: No safeguards needed
Removing the GPU
Integer-only arithmetic
Loss/gradient scaling to keep small fp16 gradients from underflowing to zero
questions.explanation: fp16 saves memory and uses fast tensor cores; loss scaling keeps tiny gradients representable, preventing underflow while critical ops stay higher precision.

questions.question: Knowledge distillation (e.g. DistilBERT) helps inference because the student:
options: Is always more accurate than the teacher
Is smaller and faster while keeping much of the teacher's accuracy, lowering latency and cost
Has more parameters for speed
Only changes the tokenizer
questions.explanation: A compact student trained to mimic a larger teacher runs faster with less memory while retaining most quality, ideal for latency-sensitive serving.

questions.question: Int8 dynamic quantization of a deployed transformer trades:
options: Smaller, faster inference for a usually small accuracy drop
Larger model for higher accuracy
No size change but slower inference
Works only during training
questions.explanation: Int8 weights shrink the model and speed integer math at inference (notably CPU), costing some precision — typically a minor, acceptable accuracy decrease.

questions.question: Classifying text into a label never fine-tuned on, using only a prompt description, is:
options: Supervised fine-tuning
Gradient descent
Zero-shot learning — a task with no task-specific training examples
Tokenization
questions.explanation: Zero-shot learning performs an untrained task guided by a natural-language description, leveraging pretrained knowledge without labeled examples or weight updates.

questions.question: Few-shot in-context learning uses prompt examples by:
options: Updating the model's weights
Ignoring them
Storing them in the tokenizer
Conditioning on them at inference to infer and apply the pattern, with no weight updates
questions.explanation: Demonstrations in the prompt let the model recognize the desired pattern and answer the query purely via the forward pass, with no gradient updates.

questions.question: Multi-task learning with a shared encoder benefits because:
options: Each task needs a separate model
Shared representations enable positive transfer between related tasks and better data efficiency
Every task reaches identical accuracy
Task-specific outputs are unnecessary
questions.explanation: A shared encoder learns cross-task features so related tasks reinforce each other and use data/parameters efficiently, while task heads produce each output.

questions.question: A Vision Transformer turns an image into a sequence by:
options: Splitting it into fixed-size patches, linearly embedding each, and treating patches as tokens
Feeding raw pixels one by one
Running OCR to text first
Using no embeddings
questions.explanation: ViT divides the image into patches, projects each into an embedding with positional info, and processes these patch tokens with a standard transformer encoder.

questions.question: Efficient/long-context transformers primarily change full attention by:
options: Removing attention for convolutions
Shortening inputs to 8 tokens
Approximating or sparsifying it so cost scales sub-quadratically, enabling longer sequences
Doubling the quadratic cost
questions.explanation: Full attention is O(n^2); sparse/low-rank/linear approximations reduce cost toward linear or n-log-n, allowing much longer contexts.

questions.question: Live accuracy slowly declines for months with unchanged code. The likely cause and fix:
options: A code bug; rewrite inference
Wrong server clock; restart
Corrupted model file; re-download
Data/concept drift — monitor inputs/predictions and retrain on recent data
questions.explanation: Silent decay with unchanged code signals distribution drift; monitoring detects it and retraining on fresh, representative data restores performance.

questions.question: With abundant unlabeled in-domain text but few in-domain labels, the best capstone strategy is:
options: Train from scratch on only the labels
Domain-adaptive continued pretraining on the unlabeled text, then fine-tune on the labels
Discard the unlabeled text
Use a smaller vocabulary
questions.explanation: Continuing MLM pretraining on in-domain text teaches domain vocabulary/style, then fine-tuning on scarce labels adapts to the task — far better than label-only training.
